commonIssues: Frost tender: leaves and fruit are damaged below about 0 C (32 F) and the tree suffers significant injury below roughly -4 C (25 F); in climates colder than USDA zone 8b/9a grow in a container and overwinter indoors, in a greenhouse, conservatory or glazed porch (RHS, NC State Extension).

Overwatering / waterlogging is a primary killer: lemons dislike soggy roots. Let the top 2-3 inches of soil dry, then water thoroughly; never let the root ball fully dry out or stay saturated (Clemson HGIC, UMD Extension).

Container note: a lemon is naturally a 6-10 ft tree. The recommended minimum here (~26 L, a 14 in pot) is a starting size for a small or dwarf tree; pot up gradually toward 45-90 L (18-24 in) as it grows. In-ground in zones 9-11 it needs far more root room than any container (Clemson HGIC, RHS).

Container plants leach nutrients quickly under frequent watering; use a slow-release citrus feed (e.g. 12-6-6 or 12-4-6) and feed only during active growth, stopping by early fall (UMD Extension, Clemson HGIC).
Needs high light: 6 hours minimum, 8-12 hours ideal. Insufficient light causes poor fruiting and leggy growth (UMD Extension, NC State Extension).

source.citation: RHS - How to grow citrus (growing guide). Lemons tolerate a winter minimum 'as low as 5C (42F)'; citrus 'won't reliably survive winters outdoors' in the UK and are grown in containers brought under cover; water regularly in summer (compost dries quickly), let surface partially dry in winter; feed with a summer citrus feed late March-October and a balanced winter feed November-mid March; add up to 20% sharp sand/grit for drainage.
RHS - How to grow citrus. Feeding cadence (summer high-nitrogen citrus feed late March-October; balanced winter feed) and drainage/soil-mix guidance.

Clemson Cooperative Extension HGIC - Container Citrus Production. Container diameter 18-36 inches at maturity, pot up ~2 inches wider each step; near-daily watering in hot dry periods, check top 2-3 inches, never fully dry or waterlogged; slow-release 12-6-6 or 12-4-6, 2-3 applications per growing season; optimum 65-85F, most species damaged below 25F.
Clemson Cooperative Extension HGIC - Container Citrus Production. Watering: near-daily in hot dry periods, irrigate when top 2-3 inches dry, never let root ball fully dry or stay saturated.

University of Maryland Extension - Growing Dwarf Citrus. Light: at least 6 hours direct sun, optimal 8-12 hours; fertilize with 2-1-1 or 3-1-1 N-P-K at least three times per growing season (spring, early summer, late summer), only during active growth; daytime 65-75F / night 55-65F preferred; most citrus damaged below 32F; outdoor summer plants may need daily watering.

University of Maryland Extension - Growing Dwarf Citrus. Feeding frequency (~3 times per growing season, active-growth only) and watering (daily in summer outdoors; indoors check top 2 inches once or twice weekly).

NC State Extension Gardener Plant Toolbox - Citrus x limon 'Meyer'. Full sun (6+ hours) preferred for fruit production; small broadleaf evergreen tree, erect habit, 6-10 ft tall x 4-8 ft wide; USDA zones 8b-11b; cannot survive frosts, grow in containers and bring indoors below zone 8b/9a; well-drained acid-to-neutral soil, prefers drying out then a good watering.
NC State Extension Gardener Plant Toolbox - Citrus x limon 'Meyer'. Frost/cold: 'cannot survive frosts', container-overwinter below zone 8b/9a; light: full sun 6+ hours best.

Confidence note: heatStressC (38 C) is a conservative estimate. Clemson HGIC gives an optimum fruiting range of 65-85F (18-29 C) but does not state an explicit upper heat-stress threshold for lemon; 38 C is a conservative typical citrus heat-stress figure pending an explicit extension value. Watering baseIntervalDays (3) and hotWeatherMultiplier (0.4) are derived from qualitative 'near-daily in hot dry periods / let top 2-3 inches dry' guidance rather than a published fixed interval. Container recommendedMinLiters (26 L) is converted from the 14-16 inch starter pot referenced for small/dwarf trees; idealMin/Max (45-90 L) map to the 18-24 inch / up-to-36 inch range. productiveMonths reflect the typical northern-hemisphere winter lemon harvest and is approximate.
